sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture and biosecurity departments, the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archive, and horticultural research.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing guava are the same: a warm, frost-free spot in full sun, free-draining soil, a named variety, and a plan for fruit fly. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Most guava sold in Australia is the common or tropical guava (Psidium guajava), and named varieties differ mainly in flesh colour, sweetness and size. Hawaiian is a large, yellow-skinned, pink-fleshed type; Mexican Cream has creamy white, very sweet flesh prized for eating fresh; China Pear is a crisp, white-fleshed, pear-shaped guava; and Thai, Indian (Amman) and Egyptian Long Neck are large dessert types. Strawberry guava and cherry guava are a different species (Psidium cattleianum), with smaller red or yellow fruit, a tarter flavour, and a little more cold tolerance. Pineapple guava, or feijoa, is not a guava at all but a separate species (see our feijoa guide). Decide whether you want a sweet dessert guava or a tart jam-and-juice type, then pick a named variety to match.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Guava is self-fertile, so a single tree will set a crop on its own. The flowers are mostly worked by bees, and active bees in the garden (or a second tree nearby) lift fruit set and fruit size, but neither is essential. If you only have room for one tree, you will still get fruit.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Guava wants full sun and free-draining soil, and it is one of the most soil-tolerant fruit trees there is, cropping on everything from heavy clay to light sand and across a wide pH range of about 4.5 to 8.2. What it will not take is waterlogging: it copes with a week or two of wet feet but prolonged flooding kills it, so avoid spots where water pools and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy. The one hard limit is cold. Guava is frost-tender, and young trees are damaged by even a light frost (below about minus 2 degrees Celsius), so choose a warm, sheltered, frost-free position (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Established guavas are notably drought-hardy, but they fruit far better with water through flowering and fruiting, so water deeply in hot, dry spells while the tree is carrying a crop. Guava is a regular feeder that responds well to feeding through the warm growing season: feed from spring to autumn, splitting the year's feed across several applications to match its flushes of growth and fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Guava flowers and fruits in warm weather, so in most of the country the season runs from late spring through autumn, while in the tropical north trees can crop for much of the year in two or more flushes. Guava is climacteric, which means it keeps ripening after picking: you can pick the fruit mature but still firm and let it soften indoors, which also beats the birds and the fruit fly to it. A guava is ripe when the skin colours up, the fruit gives slightly to a gentle press, and it smells strongly sweet.

sections.heading: Eating and storing
sections.body: Ripe guava is eaten fresh, skin and seeds and all, or made into jam, jelly, juice, paste and cordial, and it is very high in vitamin C. The catch is shelf life: ripe guava is highly perishable and bruises easily, so it does not keep or travel well, which is exactly why good fresh guava is hard to buy and worth growing your own. Pick as you need it, or pick firm and ripen a few at a time.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named guava grown from a cutting or graft rather than an unnamed seedling. Guava does not come true from seed, so seedlings give unpredictable fruit and take longer to bear, while a named, propagated tree fruits sooner (often within two to three years) and to type. One responsible note: strawberry and cherry guava (Psidium cattleianum) self-seed freely and have naturalised as environmental weeds in warm coastal districts, so pick up fallen fruit and do not plant them next to bushland. The in-stock table above shows the named guava varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Do you need two guava trees to get fruit?
faqs.a: No. Guava is self-fertile, so a single tree will fruit on its own. Bees do most of the pollinating, and a second tree or active bees nearby will improve fruit set and size, but one tree is enough.

faqs.q: How long until a guava tree fruits?
faqs.a: A named guava grown from a cutting or graft usually starts bearing within about two to three years. Seedlings take longer and, because guava does not come true from seed, give unpredictable fruit.

faqs.q: Can you grow guava from seed?
faqs.a: You can, and it germinates easily, but seedlings do not come true to type, so the fruit is a lottery and the tree is slower to bear. For known fruit, buy a named cutting-grown or grafted variety.

faqs.q: Is strawberry guava the same as common guava?
faqs.a: No. Strawberry guava and cherry guava are a different species (Psidium cattleianum), with smaller, tarter red or yellow fruit and a little more cold tolerance. Pineapple guava (feijoa) is different again, a separate species entirely.

WA.intro: Growing guava in Western Australia. Guava is frost-tender, so in WA it suits the frost-light Perth coast with summer water, the warm subtropical Gascoyne around Carnarvon, and the tropical Kimberley in the north.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Guava needs warmth and hates frost, which shapes where it grows in WA. Perth has a mild Mediterranean climate with only light frosts, so guava grows well in a sunny, sheltered spot given summer irrigation. It is more at home in the warm subtropical Gascoyne and in the tropical north, while cold, frost-prone inland and southern districts are marginal.

sections.heading: Where guava grows in WA
sections.body: The reliable areas are warm. Around Perth, guava is a common backyard tree in frost-free pockets. The Gascoyne district around Carnarvon has a subtropical climate that ripens tropical fruit such as bananas and mangoes, and guava grows there too. Furthest north, the Ord River irrigation country around Kununurra, in the Kimberley, is true tropics and crops guava strongly.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: In the tropical Kimberley, guava follows the wet-dry tropical pattern and can flush more than once a year, while around Perth and the south it is mainly an autumn crop. Pick fruit mature but firm and ripen it indoors to stay ahead of fruit fly.

sections.heading: Fruit fly in WA
sections.body: WA's fruit fly is the Mediterranean fruit fly (Medfly), which is established right across the state from Esperance to Carnarvon, and guava is one of its high-susceptibility hosts, so plan to bag fruit, bait, or pick early and ripen indoors. The Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states is not established in WA: it is a prohibited pest that WA actively keeps out, having stamped out Perth incursions ten times since 1989.

sections.heading: Myrtle rust in WA
sections.body: Myrtle rust, a fungal disease of the myrtle family that includes guava, has hit the humid eastern states hard, but WA is still almost free of it. The only WA detection was a single remote site in the far north (the East Kimberley) in 2022. It is a declared pest in WA, so if you see its bright yellow spores on guava or any myrtle-family plant, report it.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually treated and certified before entry, so many eastern nurseries will not post plants here and the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA. Both common guava and strawberry guava are permitted plants in WA (unlike their environmental-weed status in the eastern states), so either can be bought and grown here.

faqs.q: Which fruit fly attacks guava in Western Australia?
faqs.a: The Mediterranean fruit fly (Medfly), which is established across WA and rates guava as a high-susceptibility host. Queensland fruit fly is not established in WA. Bag fruit, use baits, or pick mature-firm and ripen indoors.

faqs.q: Why won't some nurseries post guava trees to WA?
faqs.a: WA quarantine law requires live plants to be permitted on the WA Organism List and usually treated and certified before they cross the border, so many eastern-states nurseries choose not to ship plants to WA.

QLD.intro: Growing guava in Queensland. Queensland is the heart of guava growing in Australia, from the wet tropics of the far north, where trees crop for much of the year, to subtropical South East Queensland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Guava loves Queensland's warmth and humidity. In the tropical far north it grows and fruits almost year-round, and across subtropical Queensland it is an easy, vigorous tree. Frost is the only real brake, so the main risk is in cold inland pockets rather than on the coast.

sections.heading: Where guava grows in QLD
sections.body: Australian guava production is centred on Queensland, and the tree does well from the wet tropics and the Atherton Tableland in the far north, through the Wide Bay and Bundaberg districts, to the gardens and farms of South East Queensland. The guava root-knot nematode, a guava-associated pest, has been detected on hosts in Queensland, a sign of how widely guava is grown across the state.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In the tropical north, guava crops in flushes across much of the year, with main peaks around late spring and again in late summer to autumn. Further south in subtropical Queensland the season is more defined, building over summer and tapering into the cooler months.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native and endemic here and readily attacks guava, so bagging fruit, or picking mature-firm and ripening indoors, pays off. Fruit-piercing moths, which breed for most of the year in the north, and fruit-spotting bug also damage the fruit, and cryptic mealybug can infest the plant. The big disease is myrtle rust, which is widespread on the humid Queensland coast and infects guava and its myrtle-family relatives.

sections.heading: Growing guava responsibly in QLD
sections.body: Both common guava and strawberry (cherry) guava are recognised environmental weeds in coastal Queensland, spread by birds and flying foxes into bushland. Neither is a declared or prohibited plant, so they are legal to grow, but be a good neighbour: pick up fallen fruit and avoid planting next to native bush.

faqs.q: Does guava grow well in Brisbane and South East Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. Subtropical South East Queensland suits guava well, though the season is more defined than in the tropical north. Expect to manage Queensland fruit fly and, in humid spells, myrtle rust.

faqs.q: Is guava a weed in Queensland?
faqs.a: Common guava and strawberry (cherry) guava are both regarded as environmental weeds in coastal Queensland, though neither is a declared plant. They are legal to grow, but pick up fallen fruit and avoid planting beside bushland.

NSW.intro: Growing guava in New South Wales. Guava is happiest on the warm, subtropical north coast (the Northern Rivers); frost and cold limit it inland and in the south, and Sydney is marginal.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Guava needs warmth and is frost-tender, so in NSW it is really a coastal, subtropical crop. The warm Northern Rivers and north coast suit it well, while cold inland tablelands and the cooler south are marginal, and the Sydney basin is borderline, workable only in a warm, sheltered, frost-free spot.

sections.heading: Where guava grows in NSW
sections.body: Commercial subtropical fruit in NSW is grown on the north coast, and guava goes with it: the Northern Rivers around the Tweed and Lismore, and south through Coffs Harbour on the mid-north coast. Tellingly, guava has naturalised right along this warm coastline, from Tweed Heads down to Taree.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the NSW coast, guava ripens through late summer and into autumn. Pick the fruit mature but firm and ripen it indoors to stay ahead of fruit fly and birds.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to north-eastern NSW and is the state's main fruit pest, and it readily attacks guava, so protect the fruit. Myrtle rust is the key disease: it was first found in Australia on the NSW central coast in 2010 and infects guava and its myrtle-family relatives, hitting hardest in the humid coastal districts where guava grows best. Fruit-piercing moths are a further coastal problem.

sections.heading: Growing guava responsibly in NSW
sections.body: Strawberry (cherry) guava is a significant environmental weed along the NSW north coast and is listed among the world's worst invasive plants, and common guava also naturalises here. They are legal to grow, but pick up fallen fruit and keep them away from bushland.

faqs.q: Can you grow guava in Sydney?
faqs.a: It is marginal. Sydney's cooler winters and frosts mean guava needs a warm, sheltered, frost-free spot, ideally against a north-facing wall, and even then it does better on the warmer NSW north coast.

faqs.q: When does guava fruit in New South Wales?
faqs.a: On the warm coast, mainly through late summer and autumn. Pick the fruit mature but firm and let it ripen indoors to beat the fruit fly and birds.

VIC.intro: Growing guava in Victoria. Victoria is cool-temperate and marginal for true guava, which is frost-tender, so it needs a warm, sheltered microclimate or a pot you can move, and feijoa is the hardy alternative many Victorians grow instead.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Guava is frost-tender, and most of Victoria is too cold for it to thrive outdoors unprotected. It can be grown in the warmest, most sheltered, north-facing corners of Melbourne and other towns, in the warm inland around the Sunraysia (Mildura), or in a large pot moved under cover for winter. Cold, frost-prone gardens are not suited to it.

sections.heading: Choosing guava or feijoa in a cool climate
sections.body: If you want a reliable myrtle-family fruit in Victoria, feijoa (pineapple guava, Acca sellowiana) is the cold-hardy alternative: it shrugs off frosts that kill true guava, surviving to around minus 10 degrees, and crops happily across southern Victoria. True guava is the choice only where you can give it real warmth and frost protection (see our feijoa guide).

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Where it does fruit, guava ripens in autumn, and in a cool season the fruit may not finish ripening on the tree before winter. Because guava keeps ripening after picking, pick it mature but firm and finish it indoors.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established in Victoria, especially in the fruit-growing north around the Sunraysia and the Goulburn Valley, and guava is a host, so protect the fruit. Myrtle rust has also reached Victoria, mainly in and around Melbourne, and can infect guava and other myrtle-family plants.

faqs.q: Can you grow guava in Melbourne or Victoria?
faqs.a: Only marginally. True guava is frost-tender and needs a warm, sheltered, north-facing spot or a pot brought under cover in winter. Many Victorians grow feijoa instead, which is far more frost-hardy.

faqs.q: What is a cold-hardy alternative to guava?
faqs.a: Feijoa, also called pineapple guava (Acca sellowiana). It is a separate species that tolerates frost far better than true guava (to around minus 10 degrees) and crops well across southern Victoria.
